source: kb/Engineering + CTO Office/2025-03-18_Olympus SaaS Check-in - Monthly (4).txt
content:
- Olympus SaaS Check-in – Ramki Gaddipati’s Office Hours (Digital Twin Structured Summary)

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- This was a monthly cross-functional review and planning meeting for Olympus SaaS initiatives, driven by updates from engineering, product, and SRE teams. The agenda included reviewing the status of February and March release items, assessing progress on features (Teleport, World Pass, external flows onboarding), and evaluating dashboards, incident apps, and module management tooling (Publisher Home). Ramki’s role throughout was to clarify ambiguous statuses, reinforce focus on user-centric language and UX, and ensure clarity and practicality in technical and process implementation. Several discussions revolved around should-we/shouldn’t-we status of features, usability blockers, nomenclature issues, and a strong emphasis on not exposing internal implementation complexity (e.g., deployment vs. version, config storage abstraction) to end users or customers.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Teleport and World Pass Status Clarified:
- Teleport is considered abandoned pending a clear use case versus World Pass; its effort is paused pending further decision after upcoming discussions.
(e.g., “we at the moment... don’t have a valid use case... once we conclude on World Pass”)
- Metrics and External Flows Onboarding Partially Complete:
- Metric onboarding for egress flows is in progress, with some changes still pending and a need for further explanation to Ramki.
- Hippo App Teams Integration Facing Stability and UX Issues:
- Teams tab crashing, interface inconsistencies, and a request from Ramki to make the Hippo app independently usable outside Teams and integrate incident lists and filters directly for better usability ("should be completely clickable… why two different developments?").
- Weave Analytics & Performance Dashboard Not Yet in Production:
- Current dashboard has data flow and deployment representational issues; Ramki highlights gaps between technical abstraction and user value (“need to look at persona and what we’re delivering for them”).
- Publisher Home Access and Information Architecture Issues:
- Access/authorization broken for some users; UI/UX and module naming conventions criticized, with instruction to make page content and error messages more user-informative; workflow terms like “release request” to be renamed for clarity.
- Deployment vs. Version Clarity and Abstraction:
- Strong push by Ramki to distinguish deployment as a standalone entity from version/spec; not to burden application teams/developers with data storage backend complexity.
- Functionality De-scoping & Open Tickets:
- Some features (FaaS, event publication patterns) explicitly called out as deprecated or to be handled by generic features/templates ("not required as separate functionality").
- Release Notes and Change Descriptions Must Be Customer-Centric:
- Ramki emphasized all summaries, notes, and labels should be comprehensible and meaningful to end customers, not shortcuts for internal understanding.

- V. Ramki's Insights

1. Feature Use-Case Validation and Pruning:
- Ramki consistently probes whether ongoing/planned efforts (such as Teleport) are justified by a solid use case, and quickly recommends abandoning or pausing initiatives if customer value or fit is unclear. His direction is emphatic about not committing resources without clear need, seeking directness and minimalism regarding technical sprawl.

2. Strong User-Centricity in Tooling and Labels:
- Urges all release notes, module names, and UI elements be easily decipherable and directly valuable to customers. He is critical of shorthand labels, ambiguous status columns, and generic error messages, pushing for user-facing clarity and actionable guidance in every interaction point (e.g., error screens should instruct).

3. Abstraction of Internal Mechanics for Customers and Developers:
- Emphasizes repeatedly that internal system attributes (whether underlying config is in Git DB, PG, cluster spec versioning, etc.) should be fully abstracted from application developers and end users. Developers should consume simple APIs/collections regardless of backend, and not require in-depth knowledge of platform storage mechanisms.

4. Demand for Logical UX and Clear Navigation Structures:
- Provides specific feedback that deployments, version comparisons, and dependency relationships should be directly navigable by deployment/entity, not through indirect or tabular mappings that confuse the difference between a deployment and its spec. Feedback is delivered with urgency and direct problem identification, expecting UI/UX corrections promptly.

5. Complimenting Progress While Escalating Quality Expectations:
- Recognizes good initial launches (e.g., Hippo integration, developer progress on onboarding modules) but immediately follows up with targeted critiques—expecting fixes, more robust help systems, and content-rich pages. Complements are tightly coupled with further assignments for continuous improvement.

6. Insistence on Precise Terminology and Nomenclature:
- Calls out confusing or misleading terms (e.g., “deployment,” “release request,” “module names with Olympus prefix”) and requires vocabulary to be precise and meaningful, inside the product, docs, and communications, to avoid confusion among teams and users.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. Teleport Status Update:
- Task: Confirm and communicate abandonment of Teleport unless a concrete use case emerges post-World Pass decision.
- Assigned To: Vivekanand Gavini / Relevant Product Owner
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Mark as “abandoned” in project tracking if work is not actively ongoing; update status post-World Pass discussion.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: After this week’s World Pass review call.

2. Improve Metrics and Onboarding Explanation:
- Task: Provide simplified explanation of the changes needed for onboarding metrics for external flows to Ramki, ensuring Heracles support is clarified.
- Assigned To: Lakkarusu Bhavani
- Instructions: Walkthrough to be scheduled separately; be ready to clarify specifics with concrete examples.
- Timeline: As follow-up after this call.

3. Teams Hippo App: Usability & Stability Fixes:
- Task: Resolve “tab crashing” in Teams, enable Hippo as standalone app, unify incident lists/filters for direct access, conduct a thorough UX audit.
- Assigned To: Prateek Jaiswal / Hippo Dev Team
- Instructions: Eliminate Teams-specific dependencies in app HTML/JS; redesign to include all incident filtering and navigation within app; meet basic UX expectations (clickable filters, direct navigation).
- Timeline: ASAP, with UX audit to be prioritized.

4. Publisher Home: Access, Content, Nomenclature, and Help:
- Task: Fix unauthorized access, refine error/info messages, standardize module naming, and ensure every page has a help/feedback section.
- Assigned To: Aleem Javed / Publisher Home Owners
- Instructions: Authorize affected users, update error screens with actionable steps, audit “Olympus” prefixing issue in module names, and include contextual help everywhere.
- Timeline: Immediate for access, with nomenclature/help audits as continuous improvements.

5. Deployment Snapshot and Comparison UX:
- Task: Reorient dashboard/UI to treat deployments as first-class “entities” (with unique IDs), allow grouping/filtering by deployment, not by version/spec; review tabular layouts, apply clear navigation/drilldown, and validate with real user stories.
- Assigned To: Aleem Javed, Eswar Kumar Perabathini, and associated dashboard teams
- Instructions: Rethink data organization, separate deployment objects, and test new UI flows with stakeholders.
- Timeline: Incorporate into next design/UX cycle; bring updated mocks to next discussion.

- VII. Jira Ticket

- Scope: “Teams Hippo App Stability and UX Overhaul”
- Bugfix: Tab/app crashing;
- Enhancement: Standalone windowing;
- Feature: Unified incident filtering and navigation;
- UX: Full audit and corrections based on Ramki’s review.

- Assignee: Prateek Jaiswal / Hippo App Engineering Team

- Justification:
- Primary customer and internal SRE interface is broken (crashes), user journeys are non-linear, critical UX flaws impede adoption and productivity. Direct CTO mandate for ASAP stabilization and redesign to meet high-visibility operational requirements.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

- Clarify World Pass capabilities comparison: Ensure that the upcoming World Pass discussion explicitly covers needed audit/logging features currently attributed to Teleport, aligning requirements before further development.
- Metrics Onboarding Deep Dive: Suggest scheduling a hands-on session for Ramki with Lakkarusu Bhavani to clarify Heracles and metrics onboarding flow, addressing existing ambiguities.
- Publisher Home Improvements Demonstration: For upcoming meetings, have the Publisher Home and deployment dashboards demonstrated live with real (not staging) data to verify authorization flow and user-facing information structure.
- Deployment Abstraction Working Group: Recommend forming a small working group with dashboard and backend teams to define and enforce the “deployment entity” paradigm Ramki emphasized and to audit where backend abstraction might leak into end-user experience.

- This summary is structured to power Ramki’s Digital Twin with precise, actionable memory of his stances, instructions, critical perspectives, and leadership philosophy as shown in the call.

attendees: Amayaranjan Das (plus mentions of additional team members: Vivekanand Gavini
